summary: DHH reviews the Asus Zenfone 9 as a surprisingly compelling Android phone after years of lukewarm Android experiences. He praises its relatively compact size, lighter weight compared to the iPhone 14 Pro, and significantly improved CPU performance over older Android chips. He highlights how much easier it is to sideload apps like Fortnite on Android 13 compared to previous versions. While acknowledging iOS still has better fit and finish, he argues Android's openness to installing software outside the official store represents a more important advantage than the polish gap.
key_points: The Zenfone 9 stands out for being smaller and lighter than most Android phones, making it feel better in hand than the heavier iPhone 14 Pro
Android CPU performance has improved significantly but still lags far behind Apple's chips — the Snapdragon 8+ Gen 1 scores 115 on Speedometer vs Apple's 365-400
Sideloading apps like Fortnite on Android 13 is dramatically easier than it was on Android 11, showing meaningful progress in openness
iOS ecosystem lock-in through iMessage, calendars, and photos remains the biggest barrier to switching, not hardware quality
The freedom to install software outside a curated app store matters more than the remaining quality gap between Android and iOS
money_quotes: The fidelity gap is real, but the freedom gap is bigger.
There's something deeply appealing about having a phone where at least it's actually possible to install Forbidden Software, like Fortnite, without wild interventions like jailbreaking.
It's also the first Android phone where the CPU doesn't feel like a total drag when it comes to web stuff.
Still miles off what Apple can do, but less noticeable day-to-day.
iMessage is the big one, but calendars, photos, and the other service hooks still make it difficult to just casually switch back and forth.
key_insight: Android's freedom to sideload software outside curated app stores represents a more meaningful advantage over iOS than the remaining gap in polish and performance.
